text: The robotic surgical system performs lateral pelvic lymph node dissection in a patient with locally advanced rectal cancer. The surgeon extends lateral peritoneal cuts over the external iliac vessels, which are identified and dissected completely. The ureter is then mobilized medially to aid in nodal clearance.

The surgeon creates a paravesical space to access the nodal zone between the obturator internus muscle and the internal iliac vasculature. The obliterated umbilical artery is traced down to the internal iliac artery. Fibrofatty tissue containing nodes is reflected medially from the external iliac vessels, and the nodal tissue is carefully separated from the obturator internus muscle.

The surgeon separates the nodal tissue from the bladder fat pad and dissects it away from the confluence of the external and internal iliac vessels. The final step involves dissecting the nodal tissue from the obturator nerve to complete the nodal clearance.

The surgeon carefully dissects the node-bearing fatty tissue from the branches of the internal iliac artery, preserving the hypogastric plexus. The dissection extends below the inferior vesicle artery due to the planned total pelvic exenteration for advanced malignancy.
